article_title: Tokyo Researchers Diffract a Positronium Beam Through Graphene, Confirming the Antimatter Atom Behaves as a Single Quantum Wave

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: Tokyo Researchers Diffract a Positronium Beam Through Graphene, Confirming the Antimatter Atom Behaves as a Single Quantum Wave
content_preview.summary: A Tokyo University of Science team has observed first-order matter-wave diffraction of positronium passing through a few-layer graphene grating, the first direct evidence that the short-lived electron-positron atom behaves as one coherent quantum object and a step toward gravity tests on antimatter.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A team at Tokyo University of Science has, for the first time, directly observed matter-wave diffraction in positronium, the exotic two-body atom made of an electron and its antimatter twin, the positron. The researchers fired an energy-tunable positronium beam through a sheet of two-to-three-layer graphene and detected a first-order diffraction peak at the position predicted for a single de Broglie wave, as reported in [Nature Communications](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-...

editor_notes.content_quality: Solid physics piece (787 words). All technical specifics (positronium, graphene grating, 3.3 keV energy, first-order diffraction, Nagashima quote verbatim) verified against snapshots. One framing concern about article-vs-news timing.

source_verification.https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-0: source-0.html — Nature Communications paper 'Observation of positronium diffraction', datePublished 2025-12-23. Citation authors: Nagata, Mikami, Zafar, Nagashima (article correctly identifies these names; Nagashima is the senior/PI, Nagata is first listed in citation metadata).
source_verification.https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/20: source-1.html — ScienceDaily summary dated April 28, 2026. Verified: 'two-to-three-layer graphene sheet', diffraction pattern detection, two future directions (non-destructive material surfaces and antimatter gravity tests).
source_verification.https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases: source-2.html — EurekAlert/Tokyo Univ of Science release. Verified verbatim: Nagashima quote 'Positronium is the simplest atom composed of equal-mass constituents, and until it self-annihilates, it behaves as a neutral atom in a vacuum. Now, for the first time, we have observed quantum interference of a positronium beam, which can pave the way for new research in fundamental physics using positronium', 3.3 keV beam energy, 'two-to-three-layer graphene sheet', position-sensitive detector.
source_verification.https://phys.org/news/2026-01-positroniu: source-3.html — Phys.org coverage, datePublished 2026-01-19 (January 19, 2026). The URL slug '2026-01-positronium-quantum.html' reflects this January date. The phys.org article was published roughly four months before the article's stated 'late April news cycle' — only the ScienceDaily piece is from April 28.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All scientific specifics verified verbatim. Nagashima quote, 3.3 keV beam energy, graphene target, first-order diffraction peak, two future directions — all check out. Author list slightly reorders Nagashima to first position but he is the PI/senior author so the framing is acceptable.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral. Honest about caveats (diffraction not interferometry, current energy regime not gravity-test ready, needs independent reproduction).
editor_notes.originality: Not previously covered. Cross-reference to CERN BASE antimatter-by-truck article is appropriate continuity.
concerns: Timing framing: the underlying Nature Communications paper was published December 23, 2025, and phys.org covered it January 19, 2026. The article's framing of this as a late-April 2026 News piece relies on the ScienceDaily April 28 retrospective. The story is genuine and the science is correct, but readers might infer this is a brand-new result rather than a four-month-old paper getting a second wave of coverage.
recommendations: Optional: clarify in the lead that Nature Communications published the paper in December 2025 and that the late-April 2026 ScienceDaily summary brought the result to wider attention. The article already says 'News coverage of the result reached general audiences in late April', which is technically accurate, but the lead 'Tokyo Researchers Diffract a Positronium Beam' presents-tense framing could be tightened.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Science is correct, sources verbatim, conservative caveats. The only concern is presenting four-month-old research as fresh news, which is a framing issue rather than a fabrication. Non-blocking.
